speaker: interviewer
text: March 21, 1996. Subject of interview, Mr. Walter Rockler. Interviewer, Bruce Einhorn. City, Washington, DC. Country, USA. Language, English. [LAUGHS] Well, today is March 21, 1996 here in Washington, DC. I'm Bruce Einhorn, the interviewer, and our interviewee is Walter J. Rockler. And we'll be conversing in English today about Mr. Rockler's experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. We're going to read the slate again because of an accidental misspelling. The date is March 21, 1996. The place, Washington, DC, in the United States. The subject of the interview, Mr. Walter Rockler. The interviewer, Bruce Einhorn. The language of the interview, English. Well, good morning. Please state and spell your name, if you would.

speaker: interviewee
text: Walter Rockler, R-O-C-K-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where you now reside?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Washington suburbs, Bethesda, Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: Minneapolis, Minnesota.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 25, 1920, which was a memorable day because it was also Thanksgiving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nathan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he have the name Rockler, or was it changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. It's the original name, and I think it's Rumanian-derived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when and where your dad was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a pretty good idea. I think he was born in a town-- relatively small town-- on the Russian-Rumanian border called 'Falashti,' F-A-L-A-S-H-T-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because he doesn't remember-- he did not remember. I don't even think he had a birth certificate. He was-- it was probably 1898 or 1899.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his occupation here in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: All his life he was a furrier.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your father die?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been trying to recollect that. I think around 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago, Illinois.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the cause of his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lung cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother-- what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depends on what spelling you prefer. The original Russian name was-- her first name was Evelyn [RUSSIAN]. Don't ask me to spell it. It was shortened when she came to the United States to [? Terfanski. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born, I think in, Belz, then-czarist Russia. It's the regional capital for the area, which is Moldavia. It's Kishinev, and it was some kilometers away from Kishinev. It itself is a fair-sized town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have to work outside the home here in the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. When she first came to the United States, she worked in department stores in Minneapolis and Chicago. And then for a long period of time when she had children, she stayed at home, and then she worked with my father in his business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is your mom now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's still alive. She's in Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were either of your parents Holocaust survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were both in the United States before World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: A younger-- younger sister and brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when they were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sure do, 1925 and 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the brother the eldest or the sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm the eldest, my sister is next, and my brother is the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Iris. Schoenfeld is her married name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they're both alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they active as participants in the Nuremberg proceedings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did you live in Minneapolis after your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you and your family move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you continue to reside in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, through my college years, which would mean up to about the age of 19, and then more recently, between 1954 and 1966, I practiced law there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of town did you grow up in in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: A semi-slum area on the west side, which was basically the Jewish community there. But at one time or another I've lived in almost every part of Chicago, including the North Shore suburbs, which are relatively affluent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your family's religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, but not intensely religious at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a local synagogue that you visited a lot, or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I've never had any synagogue that I visited a lot. But I did have a bar mitzvah when I was young, and if the affiliation was anything, it was orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to high school in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school, Von Steuben. Von Steuben.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you graduate?

speaker: interviewee
text: High school? 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, as you were entering adulthood, as you were going through high school, did you have any particular career goals?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was really very simple. I just went to classes regularly and scored. That was what it was all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: No particular--

speaker: interviewee
text: Winning. Winning.

speaker: interviewer
text: No particular ambitions at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have-- I hadn't any well-defined talents, and I had no well-defined ambitions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend college?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: University of Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a particular concentration of study?

speaker: interviewee
text: I concentrated on whatever [LAUGHS] the studies were. No, I had a scholarship there, and for the first two years of the University of Chicago, you had a-- practically a fixed curriculum. This was in the days of Robert Maynard Hutchins as president and chancellor, and it was diversified. But-- but-- but my area of specialization was so-called 'social studies department,' and my emphasis was heavily on political science and economics-- what is called 'political science.' It's political, all right, but not so science.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live at the university, or did you commute from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked from home. We-- I lived within about a mile and a half, and I walled back and forth every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was chilly, very chilly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did you like college?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my career. I mean, my specialty when I was young was going to school. And that's what I did. I generally took twice the required number of courses, so I finished in three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the first member of your family to have attended a university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was the first member of my extended family to attend the university, I mean, among all my cousins, who were numerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandmother lived with us, so-- and she never bothered to learn English. She lived in the United States for 40 years. She understood some, but she never spoke English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maternal or paternal grandma?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maternal grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did she favor?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't favor any. She just knew one, which was Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you grow up with an understanding of Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was the first language I learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that the language--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I learned English almost simultaneously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak a lot of Yiddish at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a lot. My parents generally did not speak Yiddish, although they could when they felt like it. They did not speak Yiddish with a view to concealing anything from the kids because we knew Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My mother spoke almost flawless English, no Kissinger-type accent or any other kind of accent. She made it a point to learn to speak very unaccented English. My father had an accent. When I was young, I didn't know it. It seemed perfectly natural English to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did both your parents become US citizens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father became a citizen by virtue of being in the American Army in World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where he served?

speaker: interviewee
text: Missouri, as a mule driver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, uh-uh. He had just arrived in the States. He would-- he'd been in the States for about a year or two and didn't really speak English very well when he was in the army. My mother got her citizenship derivatively, by marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father ever serve overseas in the armed forces?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you graduate the University of Chicago?

speaker: interviewer
text: During the 30s, up through the time you graduated university, were you active politically?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a member of any particular organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, but kind of marginally in college. In the-- in the late '30s, there was a big student organization, national in character, called the American Student Union. Around 1939, it split. And it really split because there was some general feeling that the communists had taken it over at that time. This was around the time, I think-- I'm not sure of the exact dates, but I think 1940 was the year that the Soviet Union invaded Finland, which seemed to produce a lot of agitation. And I went with the split-off group, which was anti-communist, which-- which got-- I can't remember exactly the name of it, but I think it became National Student something to distinguish itself from the ASU.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your political philosophy like as a young adult, a student?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was educated at the University of Chicago. Obviously, I was a right-winger in those days. The University of Chicago never produced anything but right-wingers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a party preference as a student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my family, like all immigrant families, would-- voted Democratic. But I had great doubts that the Democrats were on the path to virtue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where were you at the beginning of the Second World War in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the University of Chicago, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what were you doing with yourself at that time? Were you still a student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, going back and forth to classes, listening to lectures, like Edward Benes, who was at the University of Chicago in '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about Professor Benes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Professor Benes had been prime minister or president-- I forget what the title was-- of Czechoslovakia when the Germans marched which was 1939. The Germans moved into Czechoslovakia in March 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he then move to Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: They quickly made it evident that his presence was not desired in Europe, and if he stayed very long, he might not even be in Europe or anywhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take him as a-- as a student?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was a lecturer. I don't know that he did any classes, but he-- he turned up at the University of Chicago, which had all kinds of interesting people. Somewhere along the-- that time, we had Bertrand Russell there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever attend any of Benes's lectures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I also attended Russell's lectures. I attended everything that was around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, just prior to the beginning of World War II, how familiar were you with what was going on inside Nazi Germany and Central Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely familiar. This was an area in which I was interested and also was part of my curricular studies. For example, somewhere around the age of 16, I read from cover to cover. And I'm probably one of the few people alive today who ever did read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have an effect on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had a pretty good idea of what the Nazis were up to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever involved in any anti-Nazi organizations or committees while a student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not while I was at the University-- not in-- not up to 1940, no. Although by that time, we had refugees turning up as students-- German Jews, primarily, who were-- had been extremely well connected in Germany. You know, these were people who had titles, who were associated with the Kaiser's court, who were well-to-do, who were more German than they were either American or Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become active in any anti-Nazi groups at any other place, in any other setting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any group that I was connected with was anti-Nazi. There was no question of that. It was-- it was after college, at the law school, that I became more active, although not tremendously active. While I was at law school, there were a bunch of Anglophiles who had an interest in supporting the British against the Germans in 1940 and '41, and I became a part of that group. The notable figure there was Bill Bundy, who was a classmate of mine, who later became a director of the CIA.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what law school was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What law school did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harvard.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin your time at Harvard?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the law library at Harvard Law School.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a particular reaction to that event?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surprise. Not altogether surprise, and I'll tell you why. But I was sitting up in the-- there was a typewriting enclave where you could type your notes. And I noticed a big stir in the middle of the library, which was unusual. This was Sunday afternoon, December 7, obviously. And a bunch of students were congregated around the central desk of the library, so I finally decided I'd quit typing notes and go down and find out what was going on. And somebody said the Japanese had bombed Pearl Harbor. Well, it was a surprise. The concrete event was a surprise. But I had started studying Japanese extra-curricularly. I was in law school in the Harvard College in September of that year, which was about three months before the war, which was probably a little foresighted. I took courses there with a fellow named Reischauer, who later became ambassador to Japan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the entry of the United States into World War II affect your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I should say it affected my education. It affected my outlook, too, beyond my education. As soon as-- as soon as the war was declared, the question was what part of the military was I going to go into? And of course, the law school took the same view. They-- the second year in law school was almost obliterated by the start of the war. I think they did not run regular examinations. If you left somewhere after the midpoint of the year, you got credit for the year even though you learned very, very little in those days. And that happened to me. Within, I think, a month of the outbreak of war. I was signed up for the Navy. I was I don't think at that time even registered for the draft, but I was a volunteer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned that when the Japanese attacked Pearl Harbor, you were not entirely surprised.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because otherwise I wouldn't have started Japanese three months before that time. I was fairly aware of the fact that the Japanese were militaristic and expansion-minded, and the United States was cramping their style. The war was an outbreak, in part, of American demands on Japan, with threats that we'd cut off their oil supplies and other-- other supplies. And I don't think anybody except people who were really much more knowledgeable than I was expected the war to break out then. There were some indications that relations between Japan and the United States were not very good at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you joined the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, historically, the Japanese had tied themselves up in the so-called 'axis' with Germany and Italy, the so-called anti-democratic forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you joined the Navy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I entered in April, but I think I was signed up by January or February.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why the Navy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Navy was running a Japanese language school, and that's what I entered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that school located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boulder, Colorado, attached to the University of Colorado.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to say what it was like. It was six hours a day of Japanese classes, three, four hours of studying Japanese, strenuous efforts and very little production. And this was true not only for me but for most of the class. There were a handful of people in the class who had a real affinity for studying languages who very quickly developed either some spoken or written skills. And in fact, in the post-war period, all of the key translators of Japanese literature, classic and modern, were classmates of mine in the Navy Japanese Language School.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in retrospect, I went to a reunion of the Japanese Language School of the Navy, and I have a son who lives in Boulder who said, what kind of crazy project was that? Why would you ever go study Japanese when the United States had literally tens of thousands of Nisei with Japanese background who could speak Japanese?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to explain to him at great length that by virtue of the paranoia in the United States and the racism that was rampant, the Nisei were out of the question for the Navy. The Army took Nisei in as translators. I ultimately wound up, as I will tell you, in the Marine Corps. And the Marine Corps would borrow Army teams, but they would not use Nisei as translators directly in the Marine Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a particular rank while in the Navy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [LAUGHS] I was a yeoman second class in civilian clothes. All during the time I was in the Navy, I never put on a-- a sailor suit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever on a ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was Boulder, Colorado. There were very few ships there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you leave the Navy?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went in the Marine Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the early part or middle of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why the change--

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked-- they asked for volunteers, and since I was not a very bright kid at that time, I volunteered for the Marine Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a reason for switching within the Navy department from the Navy to the Corps?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must-- I must have had some form of death wish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you joined the Marine Corps, where were you posted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Initially, we were sent out-- there must have been about 40 or 50 of us altogether. The class consisted of maybe 200, so roughly a quarter of the class went into the Marine Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Boulder class?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was volunteers. Of the Boulder class, yeah. Boulder ultimately must have turned out about 1,000 people, but I was in almost the first class, and it was roughly 200 people. I think the Marine group was sent to a Camp Elliott near San Diego for a period of two, three months, in which we got our limited military training. In other words, they showed us what a rifle looked like and a few other important things like that. We learned to march.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you finally wear a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. By the time I was in the Marine Corps, I had to go buy uniforms. And they were very stylish uniforms. I liked them. Never wore them much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you-- how long were you stateside in the Corps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up till the end of that year. Now, let's see. That's right, up-- up to about December. But then my group-- by my group-- I said there were 40 or 50 people who went into the Marine Corps. 10 or 12-- I think 12-- were then earmarked for one of the new Marine divisions that were being formed, in this case, the 4th Marine Division. And the complement for the 4th Marine Division, which was just being put together, consisted of a bunch of odd units here and there in the Marine Corps being reformulated in a new organization. The-- the division itself was just being put together, and to put us in some kind of standby status, we were then shipped, I think 12 of us, to an Army camp in Japanese language located near Minneapolis-- Camp Savage, Minnesota.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your duties at the camp-- Camp Savage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Attended more Japanese classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an interesting camp, because it had Army people. It also had Canadian army people. And then it had 12 Marines. And as far as I know, we were the only Marines in the city of Minneapolis. I was married, so that put me under certain handicaps, but the rest of my colleagues had a damn good times.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was I married? June of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was even before that. In Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elsie Spira, S-P-I-R-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she an American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. She went through school with me in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she go with you to Minneapolis at Camp Savage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Camp Savage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think two, three months. We were put on ice until the end of the year, when we joined the division in California. The 4th Division was formed sometime in the fall of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in California was the division posted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp Pendleton, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. It's near Oceanside, California, between San Diego and Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your wife go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived in La Jolla. I mean, she had an apartment there. But I would see her on weekends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family, your parents and siblings, react to this military activity, your joining the Navy and moving to the Marines?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had no options. I did it myself. I mean, they were presented with a bunch of facts. I didn't consult them. Bear in mind, all of this was done when I was living in-- my family was in Chicago. I was in Boston or Cambridge, Massachusetts, going to law school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't consult with them before making these decisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was nothing to talk about. They didn't have any reaction. I think they kind of expected I would do something like that. They knew I-- they knew I had peculiarities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had a brother. Did he join the service?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was much too young. He was maybe 10 years old at this time. The service wasn't desperate enough to look at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did you remain married to Elsie Spira?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the marriage end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the marriage end?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a divorce.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what theater of operation, if any, were you ultimately posted? Did you stay at Camp Pendleton, or did you move on with the division?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was with the 4th Marine Division from its inception until it was disbanded in 19-- the end of '45 or '46. So I went wherever the division went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was its itinerary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first thing we did was around the end of '43-- get on board ship to go Kwajalein Atoll, where we were scheduled to take a couple of Japanese-held islands, which we did. I remember I was assigned some duty function on New Year's Eve 1943, '44, which was kind of grim because we were getting aboard ship the next day or two. And then somewhere along the line, I had a couple days off, so I went up to Los Angeles. At that time, nobody in my family lived in Los Angeles. It was just a-- an unknown city, for all practical purposes. remember talking to the French mistress of ceremonies at some bar or a hotel who assured me-- and I liked this very well-- that I would survive the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said you went to an atoll. Where exactly was that located?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 2,000 miles west of Hawaii.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on board ship for more than a month. We stopped in Hawaii and then went further on into this atoll. The atoll was taken by the 4th Marine Division, I think the 7th Army Division. The 7th Army Division took Kwajalein Island, which was the biggest island in the group, and the Marine division took a couple of subsidiary islands called Roi and Namur. But it was a huge atoll, you know, the-- the-- which was really a-- a circular coral reef surrounded a-- a lagoon. We took this-- I think it must have been January, February 1944. Then the division left the atoll, but I was left behind with a-- with a-- another lieutenant and I think four or five enlisted men on a couple of the outlying islands of Kwajalein Atoll. And I was there when it was bombed by the Japanese a couple weeks after we took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why were you left on the atoll?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's [LAUGHS] a very good question. I didn't ask anybody. They just left me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was the occupation force. All five or six of us were the occupation force. The inhabitants consisted of maybe 300 Micronesian natives. Micronesian not being Melanesian-- they're-- they're different. As a matter of fact, the natives identified me as a missionary who'd been there about 15 years before, and I became Lockwood of the islands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just thought that all whites looked alike. And they were great singers. I mean, we would sit out on the island, and they would sing in six-part harmony. I mean, they were incredibly good singers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I am not only not an incredibly good singer, I'm a terrible singer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll discuss your singing and your Marine activity some more in a moment. We're going to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very interesting because there-- there was another lieutenant who came from Indiana, and we had cornered the regimental supply of canned roast beef and pineapple. And we lived on pineapple and roast beef all the time, which was pretty good. We had no duties whatever.
